fact: In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls just 3 days before the winter solstice - the shortest day of the year when the sun barely peeks over the horizon!
Reindeer in the Arctic are now living in almost complete darkness, with the sun staying below the horizon for weeks at a time.
December 18 marks the peak time when evergreen trees like pine and spruce release their strongest scents to protect themselves from freezing temperatures.
On this date, people living north of the Arctic Circle experience polar night - meaning the sun doesn't rise at all, creating a spooky twilight world!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, December 18 brings scorching summer heat with temperatures that can melt chocolate left in cars.
The zodiac sign Sagittarius rules until December 21, so anyone born on December 18 is a fire sign known for being adventurous and wild!
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are now in their deepest sleep phase, with their heart rates slowed to just 8 beats per minute.
December 18 falls during the time when Earth is actually closest to the sun in its orbit, even though it's winter in the north - weird, right?
Owls become super active around this date because small rodents are easier to spot against the white snow from high above.
In northern regions, the aurora borealis (northern lights) appear most frequently around December 18 due to long nights and clear, cold skies.
December 18 is when many deciduous trees have been leafless for exactly two months, making their bare branches look like spooky skeleton arms against the sky.
